title: NEP+
subtitle: A human-centered framework that lets anyone — not only robotics engineers — build interactive robots and smart devices
teaser: A framework that makes building interactive robots and smart devices accessible to everyone — designers, researchers, and students, not only engineers.
intro: Most software for building robots is made by engineers, for engineers. NEP+ (Network Empower and Prototyping Platform) takes the opposite path: it gives designers, scientists, students, and makers simple tools to connect cameras, sensors, robots, and AI — on any computer, in the programming language they already know.

why.text: The future of robots will be shaped by whoever can build them. If only a small technical elite can create interactive machines, the results will ignore most people's needs. By lowering the entry barrier, NEP+ lets therapists, teachers, artists, and researchers from any field prototype the robots their communities actually need.

sections.title: One simple idea: let everything talk to everything
sections.text: A robot is really a team of parts — cameras that see, microphones that hear, motors that move, AI models that decide. NEP+ acts as a universal messenger between them: each part simply publishes what it knows and listens for what it needs, whether it runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, or a tiny Raspberry Pi. A few lines of code are enough to connect a speech recognizer to a robot arm.

sections.title: Designed around people, measured with people
sections.text: NEP+ was built following a quality model that puts usability first, and it was evaluated the same way: in user studies, newcomers found its tools easy to adopt, with low mental workload. It has already powered real human-centered projects — from social robots in homes to human-robot collaboration in Industry 5.0 factories, where it bridges with ROS, the standard robotics ecosystem.

sections.title: A bridge between communities
sections.text: The deeper contribution of NEP+ is social, not technical: it acts as a bridge between engineers and non-engineers, letting interdisciplinary teams work on the same system with the tools each one prefers. That inclusivity is what turns technology development into collaboration.
